cards.title: mazer: AI level, rank, and maze progression contract
cards.summary: Define how player level, player rank, AI level, AI rank, maze level, and maze complexity influence each other without making the loop feel arbitrary.
cards.whyItMatters: This is the backbone of the endless Mazer loop. If level, rank, complexity, and AI skill are vague, the game cannot scale fairly or feed useful learning receipts into Atlas.
cards.currentStatus: Local progression concepts exist, but the contract still needs a single source of truth for how player performance changes maze level and how AI progression stays separate.
workBreakdown: Reset AI progression defaults to level 1 unless an account-backed saved value is intentionally loaded.
Define player level, player rank, AI level, AI rank, maze level, floor, and pace score in one player-facing and Atlas-facing contract.
Tie maze generation inputs to level bands without changing the physical screen size of the maze.
Score wrap paths as both shortcuts and topology complexity so easy teleports do not inflate difficulty incorrectly.
nextActions: Audit the current Mazer progression code and receipt payloads.
Write the level/rank formula table with change-per-level rules.
Add tests proving good, slow, failed, and wrap-heavy runs produce expected level movement.
acceptanceCriteria: Player performance can raise or lower the next maze level through a documented numeric contract.
AI progression remains separate from player progression and can be reset or tuned independently.
Bleed-off edge paths are scored as both traversal shortcuts and topology complexity, not ignored.
proofPlan: Run the Mazer progression guide locally and verify the level box wording matches the contract.
Generate a cycle receipt and confirm it contains player, AI, maze, and complexity fields.

cards.title: mazer: player-facing options and info guide
cards.summary: Turn the Options menu into a clean player guide that explains controls, compass, toggles, level/rank, and current progression without exposing internal diagnostics.
cards.whyItMatters: Players should understand the game without seeing internal debug language. The Options screen is the correct place for concise, branded explanations.
cards.currentStatus: A guide section exists, but it still needs visual polish, tighter hierarchy, and complete copy for level, rank, compass, controls, toggles, and account state.
workBreakdown: Replace dense explanatory blocks with short labeled cards.
Explain level, rank, maze level, pace score, compass, controller modes, trail fade, pulse, and animated background.
Keep developer-only telemetry, seeds, diagnostics, and internal receipt language hidden from normal players.
Validate menu and play pause screens separately on mobile and desktop.
nextActions: Redesign the Options info section into compact rows/cards.
Add copy review rules for player-facing wording.
Capture route-aware screenshots from both menu Options and play Pause.
acceptanceCriteria: Options includes a compact info section for level, rank, maze level, compass, controls, and toggles.
All user-facing labels match the current state and avoid overlapping text on mobile.
Non-player-needed diagnostics remain hidden.
proofPlan: Open Options on menu and play screens at desktop and mobile sizes.
Screenshot the exact Options surface before claiming visual completion.

cards.title: mazer: edge-wrap topology, border fill, and notch safety
cards.summary: Make wrapped border paths a real graph contract, keep start/goal/navigation aware of wrap edges, and prevent maze path visuals from entering border cutouts.
cards.whyItMatters: Wrap paths and border notches change both visuals and navigation. They must be graph-owned, not just visual tricks, or AI distance, player movement, and difficulty scoring drift apart.
cards.currentStatus: Wrap navigation exists and can snap visually, but generation, fill near triangular border cutouts, and difficulty scoring still need contract-level hardening.
workBreakdown: Guarantee at least one horizontal and one vertical wrap candidate only when legal for the current maze bounds.
Represent wrap neighbors in the graph so AI, player movement, compass, trail, and complexity formulas all agree.
Keep generated paths out of title/compass notches and folded-corner voids.
Improve bottom and corner fill so paths reach usable border edges without clipping into decorative cutouts.
nextActions: Add topology diagnostics for wrap-pair validity.
Add notch-safe render masks or generator constraints.
Capture visual proof for top notch, bottom notch, and all four folded corners.
acceptanceCriteria: At least one horizontal and one vertical wrap candidate can be generated when complexity allows it.
AI distance and route choice account for wrap neighbors instead of treating them as visual-only paths.
Path rendering respects the folded border notches and fills toward edges without leaking into cutout voids.
proofPlan: Run topology diagnostics against generated mazes with wrap paths enabled.
Capture a visual proof where paths reach the border edges but do not enter the top notch.

cards.title: mazer: crisp cyber-arcade graphics and art consistency
cards.summary: Raise the full game visual system toward a crisp 2026 cyber-arcade target: sharper tiles, cleaner borders, less blur, stronger material consistency.
cards.whyItMatters: The game feel depends heavily on the maze being readable and premium on mobile. Blurry tiles or inconsistent materials make the app feel older than the current design target.
cards.currentStatus: HiDPI backing and several green/cyan material passes have landed, but the visual language still needs one consistent high-tech arcade material system across all surfaces.
workBreakdown: Remove accidental blur/fuzz from canvas scaling, tile seams, title pieces, and UI text.
Unify path, trail, pulse, player, title, border, buttons, compass, and background under one material palette.
Use actual mobile screenshots as proof, not desktop-only visual checks.
Track the approved icon image as the current visual quality target.
nextActions: Audit every draw layer for fractional pixels and scaling mismatches.
Create a visual comparison sheet for icon target versus current menu/play screen.
Add screenshot proof gates for 390-430px mobile widths.
acceptanceCriteria: Canvas backing scale and camera scale stay crisp at mobile and desktop sizes.
Path, trail, title, border, buttons, compass, and background share a coherent material language.
Visual QA includes screenshots from the actual target screen, not vague route captures.
proofPlan: Run desktop and mobile screenshot checks with route/version labels visible.
Compare against the approved Mazer icon image as the art target.

cards.title: mazer: icon-quality 2026 visual target
cards.summary: Use the new app icon as the north-star for sharper glow, material depth, palette, and shape language across the game.
cards.whyItMatters: The icon now communicates the target style better than the in-game visuals. The game should inherit that crisp cyan/green glow, deep glass, and arcade geometry instead of drifting into muddy retro blur.
cards.currentStatus: The icon has been provided as a reference, but the game has not fully absorbed its material quality, contrast, or depth.
workBreakdown: Save canonical app icon assets for favicon, browser, install surfaces, and future Atlas/link/Stripe references.
Extract the icon palette into a reusable Mazer visual target note.
Translate icon material rules into game draw-layer rules.
Use the icon as the art QA baseline for future screenshots.
nextActions: Verify the current icon assets are wired everywhere expected.
Add Atlas-facing visual reference documentation.
Start material parity work for title, border, path, player, trail, and buttons.
acceptanceCriteria: App icon assets are saved and wired for browser, Vercel, and app surfaces.
Game materials adopt the icon's crisp cyan/green glow, dark glass, and high-contrast arcade geometry.
Atlas keeps the icon reference available for later Stripe/link/auth surfaces.
proofPlan: Verify favicon and app icon assets resolve locally.
Capture menu and play screenshots showing icon-inspired palette and crispness improvements.

cards.title: mazer: fluid controls, player motion, and path feel
cards.summary: Replace snappy tile jumps and hard-coded stick segments with smoother player motion and dynamic directional intent handling.
cards.whyItMatters: Fast timed play will fail if the controls feel segmented, sticky, or jumpy. The player should feel like they are steering through a path, not stepping through a debug grid.
cards.currentStatus: Tap/hold parity and wrap snap improvements exist, but the stick still needs a true intent resolver and player motion still needs smoother interpolation.
workBreakdown: Make stick angle represent movement intent rather than hard-coded segment commands.
Resolve intent against legal graph neighbors dynamically every movement step.
Smooth visual player motion while preserving exact maze-cell legality.
Keep single-click, hold, keyboard, on-screen arrows, and stick behavior consistent.
nextActions: Design the dynamic intent resolver before changing input visuals again.
Add QA scripts for tap, hold, diagonal-ish intent, blocked wall, release, and wrap snap cases.
Tune movement speed with pace/level formulas instead of one fixed feel.
acceptanceCriteria: Single presses move exactly once while holds continue only while active.
Stick direction is interpreted as intent, then resolved against legal path neighbors dynamically.
Wrap transitions snap cleanly instead of dragging the player across the whole screen.
proofPlan: Run live play QA for quick taps, holds, stick drags, and wrap paths.
Confirm no phantom movement remains after release.

cards.title: mazer: auth, remote receipts, Atlas, and playbook sync
cards.summary: Finish the account-backed progression loop, remote cycle receipts, and Atlas/playbook handoff without exposing internal debug surfaces to players.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer needs continuity. Account-backed progress, cycle receipts, and Atlas reports are what let the game learn, tune difficulty, and preserve user/AI history across sessions.
cards.currentStatus: Supabase auth and local proof paths exist, but remote QA, receipt validation, and Atlas consumer contracts still need to be finished before calling the loop complete.
workBreakdown: QA signup, login, logout, reload persistence, and remote progression read/write through browser-safe RLS.
Keep the game playable UI clean while internal receipt details flow behind the scenes.
Separate player progression from AI progression in storage and reports.
Define Atlas/playbook receipt validation so learning reports are not just raw logs.
nextActions: Run real auth UI QA with success and failure states.
Verify Supabase rows for player progression, AI progression, and cycle receipts.
Add Atlas-side schema validation for Mazer cycle-learning reports.
acceptanceCriteria: Signup, login, logout, reload persistence, and remote progression read/write are QA-proven.
Cycle receipts are compact, privacy-safe, and stored locally/remotely according to auth state.
Atlas can consume a stable Mazer receipt/report shape.
proofPlan: Run auth UI QA with intentional failure and success states.
Verify Supabase rows for progression and cycle receipt readback through browser-safe RLS.

cards.title: mazer: unified player-facing message system
cards.summary: Move auth and gameplay notices into the same clean player-facing message system instead of leaving raw status text on screen.
cards.whyItMatters: Raw technical messages break the game feel. Errors, auth notices, and offline states should appear as intentional in-game messages, not development leftovers.
cards.currentStatus: The need is identified and some auth messages have been touched, but every error/status path still needs to route through one consistent message surface.
workBreakdown: Route login/create/fetch/auth failures through the shared message system.
Remove raw setup and network text from visible game menus.
Standardize severity, duration, placement, and animation.
Make the message style match the existing Fawxzzy app notification pattern while staying Mazer-themed.
nextActions: Inventory every visible status/error string in menu, auth, Options, and play Pause.
Replace inline status labels with message events.
Add visual QA for failed login, failed create, fetch failure, success, and logout.
acceptanceCriteria: Login/create/fetch/auth failures route through the player-facing message surface.
Messages use consistent style, duration, severity, and placement across menu and play screens.
No raw 'failed to fetch' or setup strings remain embedded in the login menu.
proofPlan: Trigger failed login, failed create, successful login, logout, and offline/fetch failure.
Screenshot each state and verify no text overlap.

cards.title: mazer: auth gate and persistent login parity
cards.summary: Finish the player-facing account gate and persistent-login behavior so Mazer matches the useful Fitness app login continuity without exposing auth internals.
cards.whyItMatters: The game needs account continuity for progression, AI-runner history, and remote receipts, but the login flow still has to feel like a game surface instead of a debug form.
cards.currentStatus: Native inputs, remembered identity, local-only sign-out, and real account proof exist. Remaining risk is longer mobile/form soak plus production env parity checks.
workBreakdown: Keep guests on the animated menu with Login as the only gated action.
Preserve signed-in sessions across reloads without storing tokens in local app state.
Route auth errors through the unified player message system.
Verify local and production Supabase env parity.
nextActions: Run a mobile auth soak for login, reload persistence, logout, and re-login.
Verify production env uses only browser-safe Supabase keys.
Keep account logout available only from main-menu account surfaces.
acceptanceCriteria: A returning signed-in user lands on the normal main menu without re-entering credentials.
A signed-out or guest user sees the animated menu with Login only.
No raw auth/network text appears in the login surface.
proofPlan: Run visible browser QA for failed login, successful login, reload persistence, and logout.
Read diagnostics only for auth state and never expose hidden technical detail to users.

cards.title: mazer: animation smoothness and lifecycle cadence
cards.summary: Remove visible pauses, loop resets, and buffered animation stalls across title orbit, scanner, background, border glimmer, trail pulse, build, and deconstruct.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer depends on continuous motion. Any hitch during build/deconstruct or title/background loops makes the whole app feel laggy even when gameplay logic is correct.
cards.currentStatus: Several frame cadence fixes landed, but mobile soak and lifecycle-boundary jitter review remain open.
workBreakdown: Keep title, compass, background, and border animations alive during deconstruct pauses.
Make scanner/orbit loops bounce or wrap without visible reset jumps.
Profile mobile frame cadence during build, deconstruct, and pause overlays.
Record animation proof with actual route and viewport contracts.
nextActions: Run a long mobile menu loop soak.
Capture lifecycle handoff frames around goal, deconstruct, empty pause, and rebuild.
Patch any remaining timer or tween coupling that pauses global animations.
acceptanceCriteria: No visible title, compass, or background freeze occurs during maze handoff.
Build and deconstruct keep crisp staged motion on mobile and desktop.
Runtime diagnostics show stable frame timing during lifecycle transitions.
proofPlan: Record route-aware screenshots or video frames through a full menu and play lifecycle.
Compare frame timing before and after animation handoff changes.

cards.title: mazer: play-mode perpetual completion loop
cards.summary: Keep players locked into play after reaching the end by recording the cycle, deconstructing cleanly, rebuilding a fresh maze, then restarting play without returning to menu.
cards.whyItMatters: The core game loop should be endless and progression-backed. Completion should feel like a polished arcade handoff, not a reset or dead stop.
cards.currentStatus: First lifecycle pass works and QA can drive to goal, but title parity, timing polish, and longer held-control soak remain.
workBreakdown: Record completion and shortest-path comparison before the next maze starts.
Lock input during deconstruct/build while keeping compass and ambient motion active.
Generate each next player maze from current skill, score, and target complexity.
Keep Reset/Menu in pause as explicit player choices.
nextActions: Polish deconstruct timing after player goal contact.
Decide whether play mode gets the Mazer title above the maze.
Run longer held-stick tests across lifecycle boundaries.
acceptanceCriteria: After reaching the end, play mode deconstructs and rebuilds a fresh procedural maze.
The timer excludes build time and restarts only when the new maze is playable.
Player progress is saved before returning to menu or closing the app.
proofPlan: Drive a real play maze to the goal with runtime QA.
Verify post-goal lifecycle pass, fresh seed, locked input during build, and timer reset.

cards.title: mazer: iridescent player, trail, and pulse material
cards.summary: Design and tune a midnight-rainbow material for player halo, trail, and pulse while keeping the core player readable and separated from path color.
cards.whyItMatters: The player and trail need to communicate progression and AI thinking without blending into pale path tiles or becoming visually noisy.
cards.currentStatus: Runtime material helpers and tests exist. Screenshots, saturation tuning, and dense-maze visual QA remain.
workBreakdown: Keep the player core locked to a high-contrast green readability anchor.
Shift halo, accent, trail, and pulse through a controlled midnight-rainbow palette.
Prevent trail/player colors from closely matching maze path colors.
Use AI memory/target overlays without confusing them with the active trail.
nextActions: Capture dense-maze screenshots with player, trail, memory, and target overlays visible.
Tune alpha and saturation for mobile readability.
Document material rules for future progression color tiers.
acceptanceCriteria: Player and trail stay visible against every path material tier.
Trail, pulse, memory, and target colors remain semantically distinct.
Visual diagnostics expose enough material samples for regression checks.
proofPlan: Run material unit tests and visual smoke on dense menu/play mazes.
Compare screenshots at mobile and desktop widths.

cards.title: mazer: diagonal path graph contract
cards.markerName: Diagonal paths and true diagonal travel
cards.summary: Design diagonal tiles and true diagonal travel as a real graph feature before any visual-only diagonal paths are shipped.
cards.whyItMatters: Diagonal routes can unlock spirals and richer maze shapes, but only if generation, movement, AI distance, compass, trail, and telemetry all agree.
cards.currentStatus: The concept is intentionally deferred. Current diagonal controls are directional intent helpers over a cardinal graph, not true diagonal topology.
workBreakdown: Define diagonal neighbor legality and collision rules.
Design diagonal render joins that fill corner-touching gaps cleanly.
Update AI distance, shortest path, compass, trail, and receipts for diagonal edges.
Resolve diagonal interaction with edge-wrap paths.
nextActions: Write a graph contract spike before UI implementation.
Add generator and solver tests for diagonal neighbors.
Only then prototype diagonal tile art.
acceptanceCriteria: Diagonal paths are represented in the same graph used by player movement and AI.
Shortest-path and complexity metrics count diagonal edges correctly.
Diagonal visuals do not imply illegal movement.
proofPlan: Run topology tests for diagonal movement legality.
Capture visual proof only after graph tests pass.

cards.title: mazer: route-aware visual proof discipline
cards.summary: Require screenshots and visual claims to prove they are on the intended route, mode, overlay, and viewport before being accepted.
cards.whyItMatters: Several visual passes were weakened by vague screenshots. Mazer needs route-aware proof so fixes are validated on the exact surface the user asked about.
cards.currentStatus: Screen contracts exist across capture scripts. Authenticated surface fixture proof and adoption discipline remain open.
workBreakdown: Fail captures when route, mode, overlay, or viewport contract does not match.
Record actual URL, diagnostics mode, surface, and viewport beside screenshots.
Use proof artifacts before claiming visual completion.
Add authenticated surface fixtures where needed.
nextActions: Extend proof workflow to any remaining auth-only surfaces.
Require visual closeouts to cite screenContract.pass.
Keep screenshots tied to the exact requested surface.
acceptanceCriteria: Visual proof includes actual route, mode, overlay, viewport, and diagnostics contract.
Wrong-screen screenshots fail the capture step.
Future DiscordOS card updates cite proof paths or blockers.
proofPlan: Run capture matrix and edge-live screenshot workflows.
Verify generated metadata includes screenContract.pass for each proof.

cards.title: mazer: DiscordOS board discipline and completion movement
cards.summary: Keep exactly one live Mazer board in Project Feedback Boards, keep cards richly formatted, and update card state/reactions as implementation work lands.
cards.whyItMatters: The board is only useful if it mirrors real work. Thin cards, wrong placement, missing reactions, or stale completion state break the planning loop.
cards.currentStatus: The live mazer forum exists in Project Feedback Boards with rich card readback. Future work still needs disciplined card updates and completed-state movement.
workBreakdown: Use one forum named mazer under Project Feedback Boards.
Keep each card formatted with purpose, current state, work breakdown, next actions, acceptance criteria, and proof plan.
Apply failure reaction for not-done cards and success reaction for completed cards.
Move completed work to the completed workflow surface only after proof-backed closure.
nextActions: Use the board card id in each Mazer implementation closeout.
Update completionPercent and state after each proof-backed pass.
Run live readback before claiming DiscordOS card changes.
acceptanceCriteria: The live board name is mazer and is placed under Project Feedback Boards.
Every active Mazer work lane has a richly formatted card.
Completed cards receive success reaction/state only after proof.
proofPlan: Run live sync and live readback against the mazer forum.
Read back channel inventory proving the forum parent is Project Feedback Boards.
